content.text: Before you can deploy any resource of a given type in a subscription, the resource provider namespace that owns that type must be registered against the subscription. Registration is a one-time action per subscription per provider namespace — it is not per-resource-group or per-user.

Many providers register automatically when you first deploy a resource through the portal or CLI, provided your account has Contributor-level access on the subscription. In CI/CD pipelines running as service principals with narrower permissions, you often need to pre-register providers explicitly.

attrs.alt: Resource provider registration state machine: NotRegistered → Registering → Registered, with Unregistering path

content.text:
# Check registration status of all providers in your subscription
az provider list --query '[].{namespace:namespace, state:registrationState}' -o table | head -30

# Check a specific provider
az provider show --namespace Microsoft.Compute --query registrationState -o tsv
# Output: Registered

# List only registered providers
az provider list --query '[?registrationState==`Registered`].namespace' -o tsv

# List providers NOT yet registered
az provider list --query '[?registrationState==`NotRegistered`].namespace' -o tsv
Provider is active in this subscription
Wait, then state returns to NotRegistered

# Register a provider namespace
az provider register --namespace Microsoft.ContainerService

# Registration is asynchronous — the command returns immediately.
# Poll until the state becomes 'Registered'
az provider show --namespace Microsoft.ContainerService --query registrationState -o tsv
# Output: Registering
# ... wait ~60-90 seconds ...
az provider show --namespace Microsoft.ContainerService --query registrationState -o tsv
# Output: Registered

# Wait in a shell loop
while [ "$(az provider show --namespace Microsoft.ContainerService --query registrationState -o tsv)" != 'Registered' ]; do
  echo 'Waiting for registration...'
  sleep 10
done
echo 'Microsoft.ContainerService is now registered'

ARM registers the required provider automatically when you deploy a resource, as long as the deploying identity has the Microsoft.Resources/subscriptions/providers/register/action permission (included in Contributor and Owner). If using a service principal scoped to a resource group only, auto-registration will fail — pre-register manually before deployment.

Service principals scoped to a resource group do not have subscription-level permission to register providers. Pre-register providers in your CI/CD pipeline setup step before running resource deployments.

Some advanced or preview capabilities within a provider namespace require explicit feature registration beyond namespace registration. Feature registration is also asynchronous and persists at the subscription level.

# Some provider features require explicit opt-in beyond namespace registration
# These are called 'resource provider features' (preview features, opt-in capabilities)

# List all available features for a provider
az feature list --namespace Microsoft.ContainerService -o table

# Register a specific preview feature
az feature register \
  --namespace Microsoft.ContainerService \
  --name EnableImageCleanerPreview

# Wait for the feature to be registered
az feature show \
  --namespace Microsoft.ContainerService \
  --name EnableImageCleanerPreview \
  --query 'properties.state' -o tsv

# IMPORTANT: After registering a feature, re-register the provider namespace
# to propagate the feature registration to ARM
az provider register --namespace Microsoft.ContainerService

# Common error: resource provider not registered
# az aks create ...
# Error: (MissingSubscriptionRegistration) The subscription is not registered
# to use namespace 'Microsoft.ContainerService'.

# Fix: register the provider
az provider register --namespace Microsoft.ContainerService

# Wait until registered, then retry the original command
while [ "$(az provider show --namespace Microsoft.ContainerService --query registrationState -o tsv)" != 'Registered' ]; do
  sleep 10
done
az aks create ...  # retry

# If registration fails (RegistrationFailed state):
az provider unregister --namespace Microsoft.ContainerService
az provider register --namespace Microsoft.ContainerService

# Error in CI/CD: service principal lacks subscription-level permission
# Grant the SP 'Resource Policy Contributor' or a custom role with:
# Microsoft.Resources/subscriptions/providers/register/action
az role assignment create \
  --assignee <sp-object-id> \
  --role 'Contributor' \
  --scope /subscriptions/<subId>
Programmatic Registration with the Python SDK
When managing multiple subscriptions or building deployment automation, use the Python SDK to register providers and wait for completion before proceeding with resource deployment.

from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential
from azure.mgmt.resource import ResourceManagementClient
import time

credential = DefaultAzureCredential()
client = ResourceManagementClient(credential, subscription_id)

def ensure_provider_registered(namespace: str, timeout_seconds: int = 120) -> None:
    """Register a provider namespace and wait until it is in Registered state."""
    provider = client.providers.get(namespace)
    if provider.registration_state == 'Registered':
        return  # Already registered

    client.providers.register(namespace)
    deadline = time.time() + timeout_seconds
    while time.time() < deadline:
        state = client.providers.get(namespace).registration_state
        if state == 'Registered':
            return
        if state == 'RegistrationFailed':
            raise RuntimeError(f'{namespace} registration failed')
        time.sleep(10)
    raise TimeoutError(f'{namespace} not registered after {timeout_seconds}s')

# Register all providers needed for an AKS deployment
required = [
    'Microsoft.ContainerService',
    'Microsoft.Network',
    'Microsoft.Compute',
    'Microsoft.Storage',
]
for ns in required:
    print(f'Ensuring {ns} is registered...')
    ensure_provider_registered(ns)
    print(f'{ns}: Registered')
VNets, NSGs, Load Balancers, Public IPs
App Service Plans, Web Apps, Functions

The Terraform azurerm provider registers required namespaces automatically by default. In restricted CI/CD environments where the service principal lacks subscription-level permissions, disable auto-registration and pre-register providers manually.

# Terraform: register providers before deploying resources
# The azurerm provider can auto-register namespaces on your behalf

provider "azurerm" {
  features {}
  # skip_provider_registration = false  (default)
  # Set to true if the SP has no subscription-level register permission
  # and you pre-register manually
}

# When skip_provider_registration = true, Terraform does not call the
# provider registration API. You must register manually before running apply:
# az provider register --namespace Microsoft.ContainerService

# In CI/CD pipelines with restricted SP permissions:
# 1. Pre-register required providers in a bootstrap step using a privileged account
# 2. Set skip_provider_registration = true in the Terraform provider block
# 3. Run terraform apply with the restricted SP

Provider registration is subscription-scoped. When managing a multi-subscription environment (landing zones, enterprise-scale), you need to register providers in each subscription individually. A shell loop using the CLI handles this efficiently.

# Registration is PER SUBSCRIPTION — each subscription needs its own registration
# Register providers across multiple subscriptions using a loop

PROVIDERS=(
  'Microsoft.ContainerService'
  'Microsoft.KeyVault'
  'Microsoft.Sql'
  'Microsoft.Web'
)

# Get all subscriptions in the tenant
SUBSCRIPTIONS=$(az account list --query '[].id' -o tsv)

for SUB in $SUBSCRIPTIONS; do
  az account set --subscription $SUB
  for NS in "${PROVIDERS[@]}"; do
    STATE=$(az provider show --namespace $NS --query registrationState -o tsv 2>/dev/null)
    if [ "$STATE" != 'Registered' ]; then
      echo "Registering $NS in subscription $SUB..."
      az provider register --namespace $NS
    fi
  done
done
echo 'Provider registration complete across all subscriptions'
Provider Registration via ARM REST API
When working outside the Azure CLI — in a custom deployment system or when calling ARM directly — use the REST API to register and unregister providers.

# Register a provider via the ARM REST API directly
# Useful when scripting outside of the Azure CLI

SUB_ID=$(az account show --query id -o tsv)
NAMESPACE='Microsoft.ContainerService'

# POST to the register endpoint
az rest --method POST \
  --url "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/$SUB_ID/providers/$NAMESPACE/register?api-version=2021-04-01"

# Poll the provider status
az rest --method GET \
  --url "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/$SUB_ID/providers/$NAMESPACE?api-version=2021-04-01" \
  --query registrationState -o tsv

# Unregister via REST API
az rest --method POST \
  --url "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/$SUB_ID/providers/$NAMESPACE/unregister?api-version=2021-04-01"

If you want to allow a service principal to register providers without granting it full Contributor access, create a custom role with only the provider registration actions and assign it at the subscription scope.

# Service principal with minimal permission to register providers
# Instead of full Contributor, grant a custom role with only the register action

ROLE_DEF='{
  "Name": "Provider Registrar",
  "Description": "Can register and unregister resource providers",
  "Actions": [
    "Microsoft.Resources/subscriptions/providers/register/action",
    "Microsoft.Resources/subscriptions/providers/unregister/action",
    "Microsoft.Resources/subscriptions/providers/read"
  ],
  "AssignableScopes": [
    "/subscriptions/<subId>"
  ]
}'

az role definition create --role-definition "$ROLE_DEF"

# Assign to service principal
az role assignment create \
  --assignee <sp-object-id> \
  --role 'Provider Registrar' \
  --scope /subscriptions/<subId>

Registering a provider namespace gives the subscription access to the provider's resource types. It does not guarantee that every resource type or SKU is available in every Azure region. Regional availability is a separate constraint checked at deployment time.

# Resource types within a registered provider are still location-constrained
# Registration gives access to the type; location determines WHERE you can deploy

# List locations where Microsoft.ContainerService/managedClusters is available
az provider show \
  --namespace Microsoft.ContainerService \
  --query "resourceTypes[?resourceType=='managedClusters'].locations" \
  -o json

# Some resource types are only available in specific regions
# (e.g. certain VM SKUs, confidential computing, specialized GPU nodes)
# Registration alone does not guarantee availability in your target region

# Check if a VM SKU is available in a specific region
az vm list-skus \
  --location brazilsouth \
  --resource-type virtualMachines \
  --query '[?name==`Standard_D2s_v5`].{name:name, restrictions:restrictions}'

Registration is a subscription-level gate. Regional availability is a resource-type-level gate. You may have a provider registered but still be unable to deploy a specific resource type in a specific region if that type is not yet available there.

Use Azure Policy to audit which providers are or are not registered across your subscriptions. The built-in 'Allowed resource types' policy can also prevent deployment of resources from non-whitelisted namespaces.

The Terraform azurerm provider registers required namespaces automatically by default. In restricted CI/CD environments where the service principal lacks subscription-level permissions, disable auto-registration and pre-register providers manually.

# Terraform: register providers before deploying resources
# The azurerm provider can auto-register namespaces on your behalf

provider "azurerm" {
  features {}
  # skip_provider_registration = false  (default)
  # Set to true if the SP has no subscription-level register permission
  # and you pre-register manually
}

# When skip_provider_registration = true, Terraform does not call the
# provider registration API. You must register manually before running apply:
# az provider register --namespace Microsoft.ContainerService

# In CI/CD pipelines with restricted SP permissions:
# 1. Pre-register required providers in a bootstrap step using a privileged account
# 2. Set skip_provider_registration = true in the Terraform provider block
# 3. Run terraform apply with the restricted SP

Provider registration is subscription-scoped. When managing a multi-subscription environment (landing zones, enterprise-scale), you need to register providers in each subscription individually. A shell loop using the CLI handles this efficiently.

# Registration is PER SUBSCRIPTION — each subscription needs its own registration
# Register providers across multiple subscriptions using a loop

PROVIDERS=(
  'Microsoft.ContainerService'
  'Microsoft.KeyVault'
  'Microsoft.Sql'
  'Microsoft.Web'
)

# Get all subscriptions in the tenant
SUBSCRIPTIONS=$(az account list --query '[].id' -o tsv)

for SUB in $SUBSCRIPTIONS; do
  az account set --subscription $SUB
  for NS in "${PROVIDERS[@]}"; do
    STATE=$(az provider show --namespace $NS --query registrationState -o tsv 2>/dev/null)
    if [ "$STATE" != 'Registered' ]; then
      echo "Registering $NS in subscription $SUB..."
      az provider register --namespace $NS
    fi
  done
done
echo 'Provider registration complete across all subscriptions'
Provider Registration via ARM REST API
When working outside the Azure CLI — in a custom deployment system or when calling ARM directly — use the REST API to register and unregister providers.

# Register a provider via the ARM REST API directly
# Useful when scripting outside of the Azure CLI

SUB_ID=$(az account show --query id -o tsv)
NAMESPACE='Microsoft.ContainerService'

# POST to the register endpoint
az rest --method POST \
  --url "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/$SUB_ID/providers/$NAMESPACE/register?api-version=2021-04-01"

# Poll the provider status
az rest --method GET \
  --url "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/$SUB_ID/providers/$NAMESPACE?api-version=2021-04-01" \
  --query registrationState -o tsv

# Unregister via REST API
az rest --method POST \
  --url "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/$SUB_ID/providers/$NAMESPACE/unregister?api-version=2021-04-01"

If you want to allow a service principal to register providers without granting it full Contributor access, create a custom role with only the provider registration actions and assign it at the subscription scope.

# Service principal with minimal permission to register providers
# Instead of full Contributor, grant a custom role with only the register action

ROLE_DEF='{
  "Name": "Provider Registrar",
  "Description": "Can register and unregister resource providers",
  "Actions": [
    "Microsoft.Resources/subscriptions/providers/register/action",
    "Microsoft.Resources/subscriptions/providers/unregister/action",
    "Microsoft.Resources/subscriptions/providers/read"
  ],
  "AssignableScopes": [
    "/subscriptions/<subId>"
  ]
}'

az role definition create --role-definition "$ROLE_DEF"

# Assign to service principal
az role assignment create \
  --assignee <sp-object-id> \
  --role 'Provider Registrar' \
  --scope /subscriptions/<subId>

Registering a provider namespace gives the subscription access to the provider's resource types. It does not guarantee that every resource type or SKU is available in every Azure region. Regional availability is a separate constraint checked at deployment time.

# Resource types within a registered provider are still location-constrained
# Registration gives access to the type; location determines WHERE you can deploy

# List locations where Microsoft.ContainerService/managedClusters is available
az provider show \
  --namespace Microsoft.ContainerService \
  --query "resourceTypes[?resourceType=='managedClusters'].locations" \
  -o json

# Some resource types are only available in specific regions
# (e.g. certain VM SKUs, confidential computing, specialized GPU nodes)
# Registration alone does not guarantee availability in your target region

# Check if a VM SKU is available in a specific region
az vm list-skus \
  --location brazilsouth \
  --resource-type virtualMachines \
  --query '[?name==`Standard_D2s_v5`].{name:name, restrictions:restrictions}'

Registration is a subscription-level gate. Regional availability is a resource-type-level gate. You may have a provider registered but still be unable to deploy a specific resource type in a specific region if that type is not yet available there.

Use Azure Policy to audit which providers are or are not registered across your subscriptions. The built-in 'Allowed resource types' policy can also prevent deployment of resources from non-whitelisted namespaces.
For multi-subscription governance, a script that checks all required providers across all subscriptions surfaces compliance gaps in a single run.

# Generate a full provider registration inventory across subscriptions
# Useful for compliance auditing and baseline documentation

import subprocess, json

REQUIRED_PROVIDERS = [
    'Microsoft.Compute',
    'Microsoft.Storage',
    'Microsoft.Network',
    'Microsoft.ContainerService',
    'Microsoft.KeyVault',
    'Microsoft.Sql',
    'Microsoft.Web',
    'Microsoft.ContainerRegistry',
    'Microsoft.Insights',
    'Microsoft.OperationalInsights',
    'Microsoft.Security',
]

def get_provider_states(subscription_id):
    result = subprocess.run(
        ['az', 'provider', 'list', '--subscription', subscription_id, '-o', 'json'],
        capture_output=True, text=True
    )
    providers = json.loads(result.stdout)
    return {p['namespace']: p['registrationState'] for p in providers}

def audit_subscriptions():
    subs_result = subprocess.run(
        ['az', 'account', 'list', '--query', '[].{id:id, name:name}', '-o', 'json'],
        capture_output=True, text=True
    )
    subscriptions = json.loads(subs_result.stdout)
    for sub in subscriptions:
        states = get_provider_states(sub['id'])
        missing = [
            ns for ns in REQUIRED_PROVIDERS
            if states.get(ns) != 'Registered'
        ]
        if missing:
            print(f"[{sub['name']}] Missing providers: {', '.join(missing)}")
        else:
            print(f"[{sub['name']}] All required providers registered")

audit_subscriptions()

Unregistering a provider prevents new resources of that type from being created in the subscription. Existing resources are not deleted — they remain manageable until explicitly removed. Unregistration is also the fix for a stuck RegistrationFailed state.

# Unregistering a provider is rarely needed but useful for:
# - Removing access to a service for compliance reasons
# - Resetting a RegistrationFailed state

# Unregister
az provider unregister --namespace Microsoft.ContainerService

# Wait until unregistered
while [ "$(az provider show --namespace Microsoft.ContainerService --query registrationState -o tsv)" != 'NotRegistered' ]; do
  echo 'Waiting for unregistration...'
  sleep 10
done
echo 'Unregistered'

# IMPORTANT: Unregistering a provider does NOT delete existing resources
# of that type. You can still read and delete them.
# You CANNOT create new resources of that type until re-registered.

# Deny registration of a specific provider using Policy
# (prevents accidental registration of services not approved for your org)
DENY_RULE='{
  "if": {
    "allOf": [
      {"field": "type", "equals": "Microsoft.Resources/subscriptions/providers"},
      {"field": "name", "in": ["Microsoft.Blockchain", "Microsoft.MixedReality"]}
    ]
  },
  "then": {"effect": "deny"}
}'

Provider registration is a per-subscription, per-namespace prerequisite. Most providers register automatically when you first deploy a resource with Contributor access. In service principal contexts, pre-register with az provider register and wait for the Registered state before running deployments. Preview features within a namespace may need separate feature registration via az feature register, followed by a re-registration of the namespace.
